structured_choices.data_source: 10,000 high-resolution dermatoscopic images collected over 5 years from a single dermatology clinic. Metadata includes: image acquisition date, lesion location, patient demographics (age, skin type), and lesion size. Clinical photographs obtained separately from the same patient cohort where available, or from a matched subset.
structured_choices.label_definition: Gold standard label: histopathological diagnosis (biopsy result) for all lesions. Lesions without biopsy are excluded from analysis to avoid label noise. Binary outcome: malignant (melanoma) vs. benign (all other diagnoses). Clinical assessment labels: standardized diagnosis from dermatologist review of clinical photographs, performed independently and blinded to histopathology results and dermatoscopic images.
structured_choices.split_method: Stratified random split by lesion class (malignant/benign) and patient to prevent data leakage (same patient's lesions must not appear in both train and test). Rationale: Temporal split is inappropriate here because lesion characteristics are stable; patient-level stratification is essential because patients with multiple lesions may have correlated imaging patterns. Split: 70% train (7,000 images from ~6,500 unique patients), 15% validation (1,500 images from ~1,350 unique patients), 15% test (1,500 images from ~1,350 unique patients). Stratification maintains class distribution across all three splits.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (image normalization statistics, data augmentation parameters) are fit exclusively on the training set. Normalization constants (mean, std per channel) and augmentation hyperparameters are computed from training data only, then applied identically to validation and test sets. This prevents information leakage from validation/test into the training process.
structured_choices.features: Raw input: high-resolution dermatoscopic images (assume 3-channel RGB, standardized to 256×256 pixels via center crop to preserve diagnostic regions). Minimal hand-crafted features to isolate CNN advantage: images are preprocessed for brightness/contrast normalization but no texture or morphological features are extracted. Augmentation during training only: random horizontal/vertical flips, mild rotation (±15°), and small translations to simulate camera angle variation. No augmentation applied to validation or test images.
structured_choices.model: Convolutional neural network: ResNet-50 pretrained on ImageNet, fine-tuned on dermatoscopic training data. Architecture justified: ResNet-50 is well-validated in medical imaging, provides sufficient capacity without excessive overfitting risk, and has published ablations. Hyperparameters tuned on validation set (learning rate, dropout, batch size, regularization weight). Training uses cross-entropy loss with class-weight adjustment to counteract imbalance.
structured_choices.baseline: Baseline 1 (primary comparison): Average sensitivity and specificity of dermatologists interpreting clinical photographs from the same patient cohort. Dermatologists must be: (a) board-certified or experienced clinicians, (b) blinded to histopathology and CNN predictions, (c) evaluated on the same 1,500 test-set patients (lesions matched between dermatoscopic and clinical photograph modalities). Baseline 2 (diagnostic reference): Logistic regression on hand-crafted features (lesion asymmetry, border irregularity, color uniformity) extracted from dermatoscopic images by domain experts, evaluated on the same test set. Both baselines receive equivalent computational and hyperparameter tuning effort.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Sensitivity (true positive rate, recall for malignant class) evaluated on the held-out test set. Justification: In melanoma screening, false negatives (missing malignant lesions) have severe consequences (delayed treatment, progression). Stakeholder priority emphasizes sensitivity to minimize missed diagnoses. Sensitivity is reported at the operating point where specificity is ≥90% to ensure the model is clinically useful (does not overwhelm clinicians with false alarms).
secondary_metrics: Specificity at the same operating point (complement to sensitivity; tracks false positive burden)
AUROC (area under receiver operating characteristic): model-agnostic measure of discrimination ability across all thresholds
Precision (positive predictive value): proportion of CNN-flagged lesions that are truly malignant
F1-score: harmonic mean, useful summary when both false positives and false negatives matter
Calibration curve and expected calibration error (ECE): assesses whether confidence scores align with true positive probability; critical for clinical decision support
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning and early stopping are performed using the validation set (1,500 images). The validation set is used repeatedly (e.g., in k-fold validation within the validation set, or multiple evaluation iterations). The test set is held completely untouched during training and tuning; it is evaluated only once, at the end, to report final performance and construct the primary comparison.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is locked and evaluated exactly once, after all model selection is complete. All reported metrics (sensitivity, specificity, AUROC) are computed on this single test evaluation. No threshold tuning or metric selection on the test set. If ensemble methods are considered, ensemble composition is determined on the validation set; the ensemble is then treated as a single model and evaluated once on test.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Confound 1 – Clinician skill variability: Clinical baseline is assessed by multiple independent dermatologists (minimum 3); inter-rater agreement is quantified (Fleiss' kappa) and reported alongside baseline performance. The CNN baseline is a single deterministic model, so variability is not an issue; this illustens the comparison context. Confound 2 – Image quality and lighting: All dermatoscopic images are preprocessed uniformly (color normalization). Test set is stratified to include a representative mix of image quality levels (assessed by domain expert before test set lock). Robustness is evaluated by computing test performance separately on high-, medium-, and low-quality image subsets to document any performance drop. Confound 3 – Skin type and lesion characteristics: Training set is checked for balanced representation across skin types (using patient demographics). Test set is stratified by skin type to ensure representative evaluation. Performance is reported both overall and stratified by skin type to detect any subgroup disparities. Confound 4 – Lesion location and size: Test set distribution of lesion location and size is documented and compared to training set; if significant mismatch is detected, test performance is reported stratified by location/size to flag any generalization gaps.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: CNN vs. Dermatologist Performance on Melanoma Detection

### 1. Data Strategy and Labeling

The experiment uses 10,000 dermatoscopic images from a single dermatology clinic, collected over five years. The gold standard label for each image is the histopathological diagnosis (biopsy result), ensuring objective outcome definition. Images without biopsy confirmation are excluded to avoid label noise. A parallel set of clinical photographs from the same patient cohort is obtained; these are used exclusively to evaluate the dermatologist baseline and must be collected and assessed independently, blinded to both the histopathology results and dermatoscopic images.

### 2. Train–Validation–Test Split

The 10,000 images are split using stratified random sampling at the patient level: 70% training (7,000 images), 15% validation (1,500 images), 15% test (1,500 images). Stratification is applied on two dimensions: (a) lesion class (malignant vs. benign) to maintain class distribution, and (b) patient identity to ensure no patient appears in more than one split. This prevents patient-level leakage (e.g., a model learning patient-specific patterns rather than lesion-specific diagnostic features). Temporal sequencing is not used because individual lesion characteristics are stable and time-of-collection does not affect diagnostic validity; however, acquisition date is recorded and checked post-hoc to ensure splits are not inadvertently biased toward recent or historical cases.

### 3. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering

All raw dermatoscopic images are standardized to 256×256 RGB via center crop to preserve diagnostic regions. Normalization statistics (per-channel mean and standard deviation) are computed from the training set only and applied uniformly to training, validation, and test images. During training, data augmentation (random flips, mild rotations ±15°, small translations) is applied only to training batches to simulate camera angle variation and improve robustness; augmentation is not applied to validation or test images to ensure clean, unperturbed evaluation. No hand-crafted features (asymmetry indices, border descriptors) are extracted, preserving the CNN's ability to learn its own feature hierarchy.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Model:** A ResNet-50 architecture pretrained on ImageNet is fine-tuned on the training set. ResNet-50 is chosen because it is well-validated in medical imaging, offers sufficient capacity without excessive overfitting risk, and has published performance ablations. Training uses cross-entropy loss with per-class weights to counteract class imbalance. Hyperparameters (learning rate, dropout rate, L2 regularization weight, batch size) are tuned via grid or random search on the validation set using early stopping when validation loss plateaus.

**Baseline 1 (Primary Comparison):** Diagnostic accuracy of board-certified dermatologists interpreting clinical photographs. A minimum of three independent dermatologists independently assess the clinical photographs from the 1,500 test-set patients (lesions matched between dermatoscopic and clinical photo modalities). Dermatologists are blinded to histopathology results and CNN predictions. Their sensitivity and specificity are computed on the test set and reported with inter-rater agreement (Fleiss' kappa).

**Baseline 2 (Secondary Reference):** Logistic regression trained on hand-crafted morphological features (asymmetry, border irregularity, color variability) extracted by domain experts from dermatoscopic training images. This baseline isolates the CNN's advantage due to learned representations vs. engineered features.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric:** Sensitivity (recall for the malignant class) at a specificity threshold of ≥90%, computed on the held-out test set. Justification: In melanoma detection, false negatives (missed malignancies) carry severe clinical risk; stakeholders prioritize sensitivity to minimize missed diagnoses. The specificity floor of 90% ensures the model is clinically actionable and does not overwhelm clinicians with false alarms.

**Secondary Metrics:** Specificity at the same operating point, AUROC (model discrimination ability across all thresholds), precision (positive predictive value), F1-score, and a calibration curve with expected calibration error (ECE). Calibration is critical because dermatologists must trust the model's confidence scores for shared decision-making.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning and model selection are performed on the validation set. Early stopping uses validation loss; the model snapshot with the best validation performance is saved. The test set is held completely untouched during training and tuning and is evaluated exactly once at the end to produce final performance metrics. No threshold tuning, metric selection, or model retraining occurs after test evaluation.

### 7. Confound Management

**Clinician Skill:** Multiple dermatologists assess the clinical baseline; inter-rater agreement is quantified. This shows how much the CNN's advantage (if any) reflects model performance vs. clinician variability.

**Image Quality and Lighting:** All images are normalized for brightness and contrast. Test set stratification includes a representative mix of image qualities. Performance is reported overall and stratified by image quality tier to detect robustness gaps.

**Skin Type and Demographics:** Training and test sets are checked for balanced representation of skin types. Performance is reported stratified by skin type to flag any subgroup disparities.

**Lesion Characteristics:** Test set distribution of lesion location and size is documented. If significant mismatch from training is detected, performance is stratified by these attributes to clarify generalization boundaries.

### 8. Claims and Scope

The experiment will establish whether the CNN achieves higher sensitivity than dermatologist baseline at ≥90% specificity on dermatoscopic images from this clinic's patient cohort. It does not establish superiority across other institutions, different image modalities, or clinicians of varying expertise levels. Generalization limitations are explicitly documented and discussed.
